perturbation.original_text: 14.1 NEITHER PARTY SHALL HAVE ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES INCLUDING, WITHOUT THE LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFIT OR BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES, WHETHER OR NOT THE PARTY WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILTY OF SUCH. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH HEREIN, NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY, AND EACH PARTY HEREBY SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, REGARDING THE TOOLS AND SERVICES CONTEMPLATED BY THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTIBILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND IMPLIED WARRANTIES ARISING FROM COURSE OF DEALING OR COURSE OF PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.1 NEITHER PARTY SHALL HAVE ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES INCLUDING, WITHOUT THE LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFIT OR BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES, WHETHER OR NOT THE PARTY WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILTY OF SUCH. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH HEREIN, NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, REGARDING THE TOOLS AND SERVICES CONTEMPLATED BY THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty disclaimer related to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, course of dealing, and course of performance introduces a legal risk, potentially violating consumer protection laws that imply such warranties in commercial transactions. This omission could allow consumers to claim breach of implied warranty where such a disclaimer would normally be present.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific disclaimer of implied warranties, the contract fails to adequately protect the parties, potentially violating UCC § 2-316. This section requires specific language and conspicuousness for disclaimers of implied warranties, and its omission leaves the seller vulnerable to claims that such warranties were not properly excluded.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the disclaimer of implied warranties, specifically those related to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, course of dealing, and course of performance. This omission contradicts UCC § 2-316, as explained in the Law Explanation, because it fails to adequately protect the parties from potential claims related to these implied warranties. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention merchantability, and in the case of a writing, it must be conspicuous. The omission of this disclaimer leaves the seller vulnerable to claims that such warranties were not properly excluded, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 In addition, ebix agrees to pay to About a fee of [**] for [**] and [**] from [**] up to [**] (the "Application Fees"). The payment of the Application Fees are to be due within 30 days after the end of the month in which the Application Fees were generated. Such payments shall be accompanied by a report stating the number of Filled Application Forms generated during the previous month, as well as the amount due to About. Ebix will track the number of Filled Application Forms generated by About Customers.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 In addition, ebix agrees to pay to About a fee of [**] for [**] and [**] from [**] up to [**] (the "Application Fees"). The payment of the Application Fees are to be due within 30 days after the end of the month in which the Application Fees were generated.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for Ebix to provide a report detailing the number of Filled Application Forms and the amount due introduces a legal risk. This violates general contract law principles requiring transparency and accountability in financial transactions and could lead to disputes over payment accuracy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the reporting requirement, the contract undermines the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in all contracts. This duty, articulated in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205, requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of their contractual obligations. Without a clear reporting mechanism, it becomes difficult for About to verify the accuracy of payments, potentially leading to disputes and a breach of this implied duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The omission of the reporting requirement in the modified text contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text ensured transparency and accountability, while the changed text removes the mechanism for About to verify payment accuracy, potentially leading to disputes. The scraped snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties'. By removing the reporting requirement, the contract undermines About's ability to ensure honest dealing and faithful performance regarding payments, thus contradicting this implied duty.

perturbation.original_text: 15.4 This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the state where a suit is properly filed under the terms of this paragraph, being either Illinois or New York, and without giving effect to conflict of law principles. Litigation initiated by ebix shall be filed in New York, whereas litigation initiated by About shall be filed in Illinois. Both parties submit to personal jurisdiction of Illinois or New York, to effectuate the terms of this paragraph, and further
perturbation.changed_text: 15.4 This Agreement will be governed by the laws of Illinois, and without giving effect to conflict of law principles. Both parties submit to personal jurisdiction of Illinois to effectuate the terms of this paragraph, and further
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the governing law and jurisdiction to Illinois only, while the original text allowed for either Illinois or New York depending on which party initiated the lawsuit, introduces a legal risk, particularly for Ebix, a Delaware corporation located in Illinois. This violates the principles of fairness and could be deemed an unreasonable forum selection clause if it significantly disadvantages one party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Forum Selection Clauses
perturbation.law_citation: M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972)
perturbation.law_explanation: By unilaterally restricting the forum and governing law to Illinois, the modified clause potentially violates principles established in M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., which dictates that forum selection clauses are enforceable unless unreasonable or unjust. Forcing Ebix to litigate in Illinois, regardless of the circumstances, may be deemed unreasonable and could render the clause unenforceable, exposing About to legal challenges.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: forum selection clause | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you forum selection clause A contractual agreement that designates the court and location where the parties would like to have their legal dispute decided is commonly known as a "forum selection clause." A forum selection clause seeks to provide a court with " personal jurisdiction" and to establish " venue ." Personal jurisdiction is the court's power to exercise authority over a party. Venue is the physical location where a court exercises its power. Thus, a forum selection clause seeks to provide a court located in a specific location with the power to resolve a dispute. Courts have historically declined to enforce forum selection clauses, . In M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co . , the Court stated that they were often perceived as being contrary to public policy or to prevent an otherwise proper court from hearing a dispute. However, the court went against precedent, ultimately ruling that " in the light of present-day commercial realities ... we conclude that [a] forum clause should control absent a strong showing that it should be set aside. " In Stewart Organization, Inc. v. Ricoh Corp , the plaintiff filed a lawsuit at a federal court in violation of the parties' forum selection clause. The defendant, relying upon §1404(a), requested the trial court to transfer the case to the federal court described in the forum selection clause. In addressing the relationship between §1404(a) (which empowers a plaintiff to change forums, typically out of counterclaim concerns) and a forum selection clause (which prevents a plaintiff from transferring forums), the Court stated that a forum selection clause is not controlling but is a "significant factor" for a court to consider when deciding whether it will transfer a case under §1404(a). The Court stated that a forum selection clause "should receive neither dispositive consideration ... nor no consideration ... but rather the consideration ... provided in §1404(a)." In Atlantic Marine Const. Co., Inc. v. U.S. Dist. Ct. for the Western District of Texas , the Court further refined the relationship between a forum selection clause and §1404(a). The Court held that a forum selection clause shall be "given controlling weight in all but the most exceptional cases," consequently altering the appropriate §1404(a) analysis. The court made three findings: The plaintiff's choice of forum has no weight and the plaintiff bears the burden of demonstrating why the case should not be transferred to the court described in the parties' forum selection clause. The parties' private interest factors weigh entirely in favor of the court described in the forum selection clause. A court may continue to consider public interest factors, however, the Court stated that they will "rarely" prevent a case from being transferred. Other than the existence of compelling public interest factors, Atlantic Marine does not state whether other factual circumstances may constitute an "exceptional case." However, the Bremen court held that the presence of fraud and overreaching in negotiating a contract invalidates the forum selection clause itself. As such, their presence should constitute an exceptional case under Atlantic Marine . Moreover, Bremen arguably supports the general proposition that any legal theory that allows a party to invalidate the forum selection clause itself, for example mutual material mistake , constitutes an exceptional case under Atlantic Marine. An "adhesion contract" (aka "standard form contract") may also provide an opportunity for a litigant to demonstrate an exceptional case. In Carnival Cruise Lines Inc. v. Shute , the Court stated that a forum selection clause contained in a form contract is generally enforceable. Therefore, the mere fact that a form contract is not the product of negotiation and bargaining likely does not constitute an exceptional case under Atlantic Marin
perturbation.justification: The modified contract unilaterally restricts the forum and governing law to Illinois, removing the original provision that allowed for either Illinois or New York depending on which party initiated the lawsuit. This contradicts the principle that forum selection clauses should be enforceable unless unreasonable or unjust, as stated in the LLM-generated law explanation, referencing M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. The scraped law snippet defines a forum selection clause and mentions the Bremen case, reinforcing the importance of ensuring the clause is not unreasonable. By forcing Ebix to litigate solely in Illinois, the modified clause may be deemed unreasonable, creating a legal risk and a potential contradiction. The omission of New York as a viable forum constitutes a significant deviation from the original agreement, potentially disadvantaging one party and undermining the fairness typically expected in such clauses.
